title: Public `selfSwap()` can recurse into `fill()` and steal from arbitrary approved users
reason: Accepted in stronger form as F-001. The core issue is broader: `selfSwap()` already exposes arbitrary external calls from Dexible, so attackers can directly invoke `token.transferFrom(...)` without needing nested `fill()` recursion.

title: Arbitrary route calls expose Dexible-only vault hooks such as `rewardTrader()`
reason: Subsumed by F-001’s broader arbitrary-call finding. Vault-hook abuse is a plausible extension, but the snapshot only includes vault interfaces/storage comments, so the vault-specific impact is less directly provable than the token-theft primitive.

title: Only `routes[0]` is funded and bounded, so later routes can steal Dexible-held ERC20 balances
reason: Subsumed by F-001. Attackers can directly call ERC20 `transfer`/`transferFrom` from Dexible via any route, so theft does not depend on later routes or the `routes[0]` accounting bug.

title: Reentrancy vulnerability in swap and fee distribution
reason: Not supported as an independent reportable issue. The dangerous behavior comes from the explicit arbitrary external-call design, not from a demonstrated reentrancy-sensitive state invariant.

title: Unlimited token approval to arbitrary routers
reason: Incorrect as stated: approvals are per-route amounts, not unlimited. Concrete abuse from arbitrary route execution is already captured in F-001.

title: Affiliate address not validated - fees can be redirected
reason: Not a protocol-auth bypass. Affiliate payment follows caller/relay-supplied execution metadata; redirecting it is part of the submitted request rather than an unintended privilege escalation.

title: Missing zero-address validation on critical setters
reason: Admin-only misconfiguration risk, not a permissionless or realistic adversarial protocol exploit.

title: Potential integer overflow in gas cost calculation
reason: Solidity 0.8.17 uses checked arithmetic by default, so overflow would revert rather than silently miscompute fees.

title: Hardcoded Optimism gas oracle address
reason: Operational/maintainability concern, not a security finding with realistic protocol-level harm in this snapshot.

title: Missing event emissions for configuration changes
reason: Non-security issue and not supported by a protocol-harmful exploit path.

title: Use of deprecated transfer methods for ETH
reason: Low-impact compatibility issue affecting admin withdrawal or relay reimbursement only; it does not create realistic fund-theft, insolvency, or permissionless DoS risk here.

title: Proxy implementation can be upgraded without timelock confirmation
reason: Not a vulnerability. Upgrades are still gated by the existing timelock and admin proposal flow; public execution after the timelock is an intended pattern.

title: Implementation contract initialization not protected
reason: Directly initializing the standalone implementation affects the implementation’s own storage, not the proxy’s live state, so it does not by itself seize control of the deployed protocol.
